title: Signatures of Rashba-Cavity-Induced Berry-curvature redistribution in the Spin-Hall Conductivity of Semiconductor Artificial Graphene
authors: Maryam Mansouri, Vram Mughnetsyan, Armen Harutyunyan, Albert Kirakosyan, Vidar Gudmundsson
abstract: We investigate the combined effect of a far-infrared cavity field and Rashba spin-orbit interac- tion on the band structure and transport properties of artificial graphene composed of quazi-2D InAs/GaAs quantum dots. The coupling to cavity photons is modeled by constructing a complete basis as the tensor product of the electronic Hilbert space and the Fock space. Our calculations for the system embedded in a linear cavity predict the existence of both type-I and type-II Dirac points which can be distinguished by their response to Rashba interaction. Namely, Rashba coupling opens a gap at type-II Dirac points, while type-I Dirac points remain gapless. For both cylindrical and linear cavities, we demonstrate the formation of electron-photon hybrid states and Rabi oscillations between energy minibands. Multiple splittings, crossings, and anticrossings between Dirac-band replicas produce pronounced modifications of the spin-Hall conductivity, including strong anisotropy and oscillatory behavior controlled by cavity geometry and polarization. Our results show that the interplay between Rashba and cavity couplings governs Dirac-point physics and provides a route toward tunable polaritonic transport and topological phases in engineered nanostructures.
summary: This theoretical work examines how coupling an artificial graphene structure to a cavity field, while simultaneously including Rashba spin-orbit interaction, modifies its electronic band structure. The key finding is that this interplay allows for the precise engineering of topological features, such as opening gaps at specific Dirac points, which manifests as strong, tunable oscillations in the spin-Hall conductivity.
main_problem: The study investigates the combined influence of a far-infrared (FIR) cavity field and Rashba spin-orbit interaction (SOI) on the electronic band structure and transport properties of artificial graphene.
main_result: The interplay between Rashba SOI and cavity coupling governs Dirac-point physics, allowing for the opening of gaps at type-II Dirac points and producing pronounced, oscillatory modifications in the spin-Hall conductivity.
method: The authors employ a theoretical Hamiltonian approach, constructing a basis that is a tensor product of the electronic Hilbert space and the photon Fock space, followed by exact diagonalization and the use of the Kubo formalism.
model_or_system: The system is artificial graphene (AG) made of InAs/GaAs quantum dots, modeled as a honeycomb lattice, coupled to a quantized cavity photon mode. The Hamiltonian includes terms for kinetic energy, periodic potential, Rashba SOI, and electron-photon interaction.
key_observables: Spin-Hall conductivity ($\sigma_{SH}$), band structure dispersion, mean photon number ($\langle \hat{N}_{ph} angle$), and the existence/nature of Dirac points (Type-I vs. Type-II).
important_parameters: Cavity geometry (cylindrical vs. linear), Rashba coupling strength, and cavity coupling strength ($g$).
main_assumptions: The analysis uses the long-wavelength approximation for the vector potential and treats the electron-photon interaction perturbatively in some derivations.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate energy miniband dispersion for different cavity types and coupling constants, and show the evolution of $\sigma_{SH}$ as a function of chemical potential, highlighting oscillatory behavior.
paper_structure: The paper builds from defining the full Hamiltonian incorporating all interactions, deriving matrix elements for the coupling terms, solving the effective Hamiltonian via unitary transformations, and finally calculating the transport observable ($\sigma_{SH}$) using the Kubo formula.
why_interesting: The explicit coupling between spin-orbit coupling (a key ingredient in topological physics) and cavity photons (a core concept in cavity QED) to engineer transport properties like $\sigma_{SH}$ is highly relevant for designing novel quantum devices.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

FIG. 2. Energy miniband dispersion along the chosen k-space path for α = 0, 20(meV nm) and interaction constants g = 0.001, 0.4 for cylindrical and linear cavity.
FIG. 3. Energy miniband dispersion along the chosen k-space path for α = 60, 20(meV nm) and interaction constant g = 0.2.
FIG. 4. Density plots of the lowest four energy minibands in the vicinity of ‘‘K’’ point for α = 60meV nm and g = 0.001.
FIG. 6. Mean photon number in each miniband for the AG inside a cylindrical cavity.

Spintronics.note: The study combines Rashba SOI (a key spintronic interaction) with cavity coupling (a quantum-optics concept) to calculate spin transport ($\sigma_{SH}$), fitting the description of quantum-optics concepts applied to spin transport in a hybrid system.
